subject: Physics
q:
A body of mass $m$ is taken from the surface of earth to a height equal to twice the radius of earth ($R_e$). The increase in potential energy will be _______.
($g$ is acceleration due to gravity at the surface of earth)
question:
A body of mass $m$ is taken from the surface of earth to a height equal to twice the radius of earth ($R_e$). The increase in potential energy will be _______.
($g$ is acceleration due to gravity at the surface of earth)
solution:
Initial potential energy of the body at the surface of the earth is given by:

$U_i = -\dfrac{GMm}{R_e}$

Final potential energy of the body at a height $h = 2R_e$ from the surface of the earth is:

$U_f = -\dfrac{GMm}{R_e + h} = -\dfrac{GMm}{R_e + 2R_e} = -\dfrac{GMm}{3R_e}$

The increase in potential energy is:

$\Delta U = U_f - U_i = -\dfrac{GMm}{3R_e} - \left(-\dfrac{GMm}{R_e}\right)$

$\Delta U = \dfrac{GMm}{R_e} \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{3}\right) = \dfrac{2GMm}{3R_e}$

Acceleration due to gravity at the surface of the earth is $g = \dfrac{GM}{R_e^2}$, which gives $GM = gR_e^2$.

Substituting this value, we get:

$\Delta U = \dfrac{2(gR_e^2)m}{3R_e} = \dfrac{2}{3}mgR_e$

Answer: $\dfrac{2}{3}mgR_e$

subject: Physics
q: Two bodies of masses $\mathrm{m}$ and $4 \mathrm{~m}$ are placed at a distance $\mathrm{r}$. The gravitational potential at a point on the line joining them where the gravitational field is zero is:
question: Two bodies of masses $\mathrm{m}$ and $4 \mathrm{~m}$ are placed at a distance $\mathrm{r}$. The gravitational potential at a point on the line joining them where the gravitational field is zero is:
solution:
Position of the null point from mass $\mathrm{m}, \mathrm{x}=\frac{\mathrm{r}}{1+\sqrt{\frac{4 \mathrm{~m}}{\mathrm{~m}}}}=\frac{r}{3}$
$$
V=-G m\left(\frac{3}{r}+\frac{12}{2 r}\right)=-9 \frac{G m}{r}
$$

subject: Physics
q: At what distance above and below the surface of the earth a body will have same weight? (Take radius of earth as R)
question: At what distance above and below the surface of the earth a body will have same weight? (Take radius of earth as R)
solution:
The acceleration due to gravity at a height h above the surface of the Earth is given by

gp=gR2(R+h)2   ...1

The acceleration due to gravity at a depth h below the surface of the Earth is given by

gq=g1-hR   ...2

According to the given problem,

gp=gq   ...3

Equations (1), (2) and (3) imply that

g1+hR2=g1-hR⇒1-h2R21+hR=1   ...4

Take hR=x

So, from equation (4), it follows that

x3-x+x2=0⇒x2+x-1=0⇒x=5-12⇒h=R2(5-1)

q: If the distance of the earth from Sun is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>, then the distance of an imaginary planet from Sun, if its period of revolution is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>83</mn></math> years is:
question: If the distance of the earth from Sun is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>, then the distance of an imaginary planet from Sun, if its period of revolution is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>83</mn></math> years is:
solution:
From Kepler's law of periods, we know that

T2∝R3⇒T1T22=R1R23

⇒12.832=1.5×106R23

⇒R2=2.832×1.5×106313

=813×1.5×106=3×106 km

q: Two planets A and B of radii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>R</mi></math> have densities <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ρ</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>ρ</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> respectively. The ratio of acceleration due to gravity at the surface of B to A is:
question: Two planets A and B of radii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>R</mi></math> have densities <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ρ</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>ρ</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> respectively. The ratio of acceleration due to gravity at the surface of B to A is:
solution:
The acceleration due to gravity is 

g=GMR2=4πGRρ3⇒g∝ρ·R

Thus, the ratio is 

⇒gBgA=1.5Rρ2ρR=12×1.5=0.75⇒gBgA=34

subject: Physics
q: A satellite of mass $m$ revolves around the earth of radius $R$ at a height $x$ from its surface. If $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity on the surface of the earth, the orbital speed of the satellite is
question: A satellite of mass $m$ revolves around the earth of radius $R$ at a height $x$ from its surface. If $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity on the surface of the earth, the orbital speed of the satellite is
solution:
For the satellite, the gravitational force provides the necessary centripetal force i.e.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{\mathrm{GM}_{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{m}}{(\mathrm{R}+\mathrm{X})^2}=\frac{\mathrm{Mv}_0^2}{(\mathrm{R}+\mathrm{X})} \text { and } \frac{\mathrm{GM}_{\mathrm{e}}}{\mathrm{R}^2}=\mathrm{g} \\
& \therefore \mathrm{v}_0=\left(\frac{g \mathrm{R}^2}{\mathrm{R}+\mathrm{X}}\right)^{1 / 2} \\
&
\end{aligned}
$$

q:
An object of mass 1 kg is taken to a height from the surface of earth which is equal to three times the radius of earth. The gain in potential energy of the object will be
[If, g=10 m s-2 and radius of earth =6400 km]
question:
An object of mass 1 kg is taken to a height from the surface of earth which is equal to three times the radius of earth. The gain in potential energy of the object will be
[If, g=10 m s-2 and radius of earth =6400 km]
solution:
Gravitational potential energy on the earth surface Ui=-GMmR

Gravitational potential energy at a height h above the earth's surface Uf=-GMmR+h=-GMmR+3R=-GMm4R

Gain in gravitational potential energy  

ΔU=Uf-Ui=-GMm4R+GMmR=GMm+4GMm4R= 3GMm4R

Gravitational potential energy in terms of acceleration due to gravity is ∆U=34mgR

=34×1×10×64×105

=48 MJ

q: India's Mangalyan was sent to the Mars by launching it into a transfer orbit EOM around the sun. It leaves the earth at E and meets Mars at M. If the semi-major axis of Earth's orbit is a e = 1.5 × 1 0 1 1 m  , that of Mar's orbit a m = 2.28 × 1 0 1 1 m , taking Kepler's laws, give the estimate of time for Mangalyan to reach Mars from Earth.
question: India's Mangalyan was sent to the Mars by launching it into a transfer orbit EOM around the sun. It leaves the earth at E and meets Mars at M. If the semi-major axis of Earth's orbit is a e = 1.5 × 1 0 1 1 m  , that of Mar's orbit a m = 2.28 × 1 0 1 1 m , taking Kepler's laws, give the estimate of time for Mangalyan to reach Mars from Earth.
solution:
Semi major axis of mangalyan,

a Mn = a e + a m 2

      = 1.89 × 1 0 1 1 m

From Kepler's law,

T 2 ∝ R 3

∴    T Mn Te 2 = R M R e 3

∴   TMn≈518 days

∴  Required time = 5 1 8 2 = 2 6 0  days .

q: A body of mass 2M splits into four masses {m,M-m,m,M-m}, which are rearranged to form a square as shown in the figure. The ratio of Mm for which, the gravitational potential energy of the system becomes maximum is x : 1. The value of x is ________.
question: A body of mass 2M splits into four masses {m,M-m,m,M-m}, which are rearranged to form a square as shown in the figure. The ratio of Mm for which, the gravitational potential energy of the system becomes maximum is x : 1. The value of x is ________.
solution:
U=Gm2a+G(M-m)2a+2Gm(M-m)a+2Gm(M-m)2a

For maxima or minima of potential energy dUdm=0

dUdm=Ga(2 m-2(M-m)+(M-2 m)2+2(M-2 m)

(4m-4m-22m)+(-2M+2M+2M)=0;  2M=22m;  Mm=2

If the weight of an object on earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>W</mi></math>, the weight of the same object on that planet will be:
solution:
Density is same

M=43πR3ρ, 2m=43πR'3ρ

R'=213R

W=GMmR2

W2=G2MmR'2

W2=213W

subject: Physics
q: A planet has double the mass of the earth. Its average density is equal to that of the earth. An object weighing W on earth will weigh on that planet:
question: A planet has double the mass of the earth. Its average density is equal to that of the earth. An object weighing W on earth will weigh on that planet:
solution:
The formula for acceleration due to gravity is 

g=GMR2

Let the density of earth and planet be ρ, and the radius of the planet be R'. Let R be the radius of the earth and g be the acceleration due to gravity of earth and g' be the acceleration due to gravity of planet.

So, the masses are

M=43πR3ρ   ...(i)

M'=43πR'3ρ   ...(ii)

So, g'g=GM'R'2GMR2=M'MRR'2   ...(iii)

From equation (i) and (ii) we get R'=213R

Substituting the value in equation (iii),

g'g=212-23=213g

Weight is given by W'=mg'=213W

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I: If E be the total energy of a satellite moving around the earth, then its potential energy will be  E2.

Statement II: The kinetic energy of a satellite revolving in an orbit is equal to the half the magnitude of total energy E.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below.
question:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I: If E be the total energy of a satellite moving around the earth, then its potential energy will be  E2.

Statement II: The kinetic energy of a satellite revolving in an orbit is equal to the half the magnitude of total energy E.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below.
options: Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
solution:
Let m be the mass of the satellite.

The potential energy of the satellite is 

U=-GMmr

The velocity of the satellite is given by 

v=GMr

Kinetic energy is given by

K=mv22=m2GMr=GMm2r

The total mechanical energy is the sum of potential and kinetic energy. So,

T=K+U=GMm2r-GMmr=-GMm2r

Hence, both the statements are false.

q:
A body weighs 49 N on a spring balance at the north pole. What will be its weight recorded on the same weighing machine, if it is shifted to the equator?

[Use g=GMR2=9.8 m s-2 and radius of earth, R=6400 km.]
question:
A body weighs 49 N on a spring balance at the north pole. What will be its weight recorded on the same weighing machine, if it is shifted to the equator?

[Use g=GMR2=9.8 m s-2 and radius of earth, R=6400 km.]
solution:
Weight of pole =mg=49 N

At equator due to rotation =ge=g-Rω2

so W=mge=mg-Rω2

∴  WP>We Wp=49 N

So, We=48.83 N. We<49 N

The escape velocity from the surface is ve=2GmR

Ratio, v0ve=GmR2GmR=12

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements :

Statement-I: Acceleration due to gravity is different at different places on the surface of earth.

Statement-II: Acceleration due to gravity increases as we go down below the earth's surface.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements :

Statement-I: Acceleration due to gravity is different at different places on the surface of earth.

Statement-II: Acceleration due to gravity increases as we go down below the earth's surface.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
solution:
Acceleration due to gravity is the acceleration acquired by a freely falling object.

At any particular place on the Earth, the acceleration due to gravity depends on the latitude of the place and the angular frequency of Earth's rotation. Mathematically, the latitude dependence of acceleration due to gravity can be written as

geff= g-Rω2cos2λ.................(1)

where, geff is the effective acceleration due to gravity, g is the real value of gravity at the latitude without considering earth rotation, R is the radius of Earth, ω is the angular frequency of rotation and λ is the latitude of the place.

According to equation (1), the value of the acceleration due to gravity varies from place to place.

The variation of acceleration due to gravity with the depth can be written as

gd= g1-dR....................(2)

where, gd is the acceleration due to gravity at a depth d from the surface and g is the acceleration due to gravity at the surface.

From equation (2), it can be stated that the acceleration due to gravity decreases with the depth.

Hence, Statement I is true, but statement II is false.

solution:

∴94=GMeRe2×Rp2GMp

∴94=MeMe9×Rp2Re2
Rp2=Re2×14
Rp=Re2
As, Re=R
⇒Rp=R2

question:

ghgd=GMRE+r2GMRE3RE-r=RE3RE+r2RE-r

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A: A pendulum clock when taken to Mount Everest becomes fast.

Reason R: The value of g (acceleration due to gravity) is less at Mount Everest than its value on the surface of earth.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A: A pendulum clock when taken to Mount Everest becomes fast.

Reason R: The value of g (acceleration due to gravity) is less at Mount Everest than its value on the surface of earth.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
options: Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> are correct and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> are correct but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is not the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is correct but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is not correct
solution:
Time period of a pendulum is given by T=2πLg, where L is the length of the pendulum and g is the acceleration due to gravity.

As, T∝1g, so the time period of vibration of the pendulum clock increases i.e. it starts taking more time to complete each vibration. Therefore, the pendulum clock becomes slow.

The acceleration due to gravity decreases with height h h≪R above the surface as:

g'=g1-hR 

Thus, value of g is less at Mount Everest than its value on the surface of earth.

Hence, assertion is wrong, but reason is correct.

subject: Physics
q: A body of mass is taken from earth surface to the height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> equal to twice the radius of earth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><msub><mi>R</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mo>)</mo></math>, the increase in potential energy will be : (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo></math> acceleration due to gravity on the surface of earth)
question: A body of mass is taken from earth surface to the height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> equal to twice the radius of earth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><msub><mi>R</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mo>)</mo></math>, the increase in potential energy will be : (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo></math> acceleration due to gravity on the surface of earth)
solution:
The potential energy of a particle at a distance r (>Re)  from the centre of Earth is,

U=-GMemr

Initially, the particle is at a height Re from the surface. Therefore, r=Re.

∴Ui=-GMemRe

When height is increased to 2Re from surface, r=2Re+Re=3Re

∴Uf=-GMem3Re

Thus, the change in potential energy is

∆U=Uf-Ui=-GMem3Re+GMemRe=23GMemRe=23GMeRe2mRe=23mgRe as g=GMeRe2

subject: Physics
q:
This question contains Statement $-1$ and Statement-2. Of the four choices given after the statements, choose the one that best describes the two statements.
Statement - I:
For a mass $M$ kept at the centre of a cube of side ' $a$ ', the flux of gravitational field passing through its sides is $4 \pi$ GM.
and
Statement - II
If the direction of a field due to a point source is radial and its dependence on the distance ' $r$ ' for the source is given as $1 / r^2$, its flux through a closed surface depends only on the strength of the source enclosed by the surface and not on the size or shape of the surface
question:
This question contains Statement $-1$ and Statement-2. Of the four choices given after the statements, choose the one that best describes the two statements.
Statement - I:
For a mass $M$ kept at the centre of a cube of side ' $a$ ', the flux of gravitational field passing through its sides is $4 \pi$ GM.
and
Statement - II
If the direction of a field due to a point source is radial and its dependence on the distance ' $r$ ' for the source is given as $1 / r^2$, its flux through a closed surface depends only on the strength of the source enclosed by the surface and not on the size or shape of the surface
options: Statement $-1$ is false, Statement $-2$ is true.
Statement $-1$ is true, Statement $-2$ is true; Statement $-2$ is correct explanation for Statement-1.
Statement $-1$ is true, Statement $-2$ is true; Statement $-2$ is not a correct explanation for Statement-1.
Statement $-1$ is true, Statement $-2$ is False.
solution:
$$
\mathrm{g}=\mathrm{GM} / \mathrm{r}^2
$$

subject: Physics
q: The relative uncertainty in the period of a satellite orbiting around the earth is <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> . If the relative uncertainty in the radius of the orbit is negligible, the relative uncertainty in the mass of the earth is:
question: The relative uncertainty in the period of a satellite orbiting around the earth is <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> . If the relative uncertainty in the radius of the orbit is negligible, the relative uncertainty in the mass of the earth is:
solution:
From Kepler's law,

T2=4π2GMr3

∆MM=2∆TT=2 ×10-2

subject: Physics
q: Two objects of equal masses placed at certain distance from each other attracts each other with a force of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math>. If one-third mass of one object is transferred to the other object, then the new force will be
question: Two objects of equal masses placed at certain distance from each other attracts each other with a force of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math>. If one-third mass of one object is transferred to the other object, then the new force will be
solution:
The gravitational force between particles is given by, F=Gm1m2r2

∵ F=Gm2r2

When one-third mass is transferred to other object

m1=2m3, m2=4m3

F2=8Gm29r2

⇒F2=89F

subject: Physics
q: A particle is moving with a uniform speed in a circular orbit of radius R in a central force inversely proportional to the <math><msup><mrow><mi>n</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>t</mi><mi>h</mi></mrow></msup></math> power of R. If the period of rotation of the particle is T, then:
question: A particle is moving with a uniform speed in a circular orbit of radius R in a central force inversely proportional to the <math><msup><mrow><mi>n</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>t</mi><mi>h</mi></mrow></msup></math> power of R. If the period of rotation of the particle is T, then:
solution:
F=KRn⇒mV2r=KRn⇒V2∝KRn-1
⇒V∝1Rn-12⇒T=2πRV∝RV
⇒T∝R1Rn-12⇒T∝Rn+12

subject: Physics
q:
The planet Mars has two moons, if one of them has a period 7 hours, 30 minutes and an orbital radius of 9.0×103 km. Find the mass of Mars.
Given 4π2G=6×1011 N-1 m-2 kg2
question:
The planet Mars has two moons, if one of them has a period 7 hours, 30 minutes and an orbital radius of 9.0×103 km. Find the mass of Mars.
Given 4π2G=6×1011 N-1 m-2 kg2
solution:
Option D is correct.

T2=4π2GM·r3

M=4π2G·r3 T2

By putting values

M=6×1023

subject: Physics
q: The time period of an earth satellite in circular orbit is independent of
question: The time period of an earth satellite in circular orbit is independent of
options: both the mass and radius of the orbit
neither the mass of the satellite nor the radius of its orbit.
solution:
The time period of satellite is given by
$$
T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{(\mathrm{R}+\mathrm{h})^3}{\mathrm{GM}}}
$$
where, $\mathrm{R}+\mathrm{h}=$ radius of orbit satellite, $\mathrm{M}=$ mass of earth.

subject: Physics
q: Initially a satellite of 100 kg is in a circular orbit of radius $1.5 \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{E}}$. This satellite can be moved to a circular orbit of radius $3 R_{E}$ by supplying $\alpha \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~J}$ of energy. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$. (Take Radius of Earth $R_{E}=6 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~m}$ and $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$)
question: Initially a satellite of 100 kg is in a circular orbit of radius $1.5 \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{E}}$. This satellite can be moved to a circular orbit of radius $3 R_{E}$ by supplying $\alpha \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~J}$ of energy. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$. (Take Radius of Earth $R_{E}=6 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~m}$ and $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$)
solution:
Total energy of satellite in orbit: $E = -\frac{GMm}{2r} = -\frac{gR_E^2 m}{2r}$
$E_1 = -\frac{gR_E^2 m}{2(1.5R_E)} = -\frac{gR_E m}{3}$
$E_2 = -\frac{gR_E^2 m}{2(3R_E)} = -\frac{gR_E m}{6}$
Energy supplied $= E_2 - E_1 = -\frac{gR_E m}{6} + \frac{gR_E m}{3} = \frac{gR_E m}{6}$
$= \frac{10 \times 6 \times 10^6 \times 100}{6} = 10^9$ J $= 1000 \times 10^6$ J
$\alpha = 1000$

subject: Physics
q: Two satellite $A$ and $B$ go round a planet in circular orbits having radii $4 R$ and $R$ respectively. If the speed of $\mathrm{A}$ is $3 v$, the speed of $\mathrm{B}$ will be :
question: Two satellite $A$ and $B$ go round a planet in circular orbits having radii $4 R$ and $R$ respectively. If the speed of $\mathrm{A}$ is $3 v$, the speed of $\mathrm{B}$ will be :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathbf{v}=\sqrt{\frac{G M}{R}} \\ & \frac{v_A}{v_B}=\sqrt{\frac{R_B}{R_A}}=\sqrt{\frac{R}{4 R}}=\frac{1}{2} \\ & v_B=2 v_A=6 v\end{aligned}$
